name: New York City: Lower Manhattan Origins

stops.name: Charging Bull
stops.shortDescription: A massive bronze sculpture created by Arturo Di Modica that represents aggressive financial optimism and prosperity. It was originally dropped outside the Stock Exchange illegally in the middle of the night in 1989.

stops.name: New York Stock Exchange
stops.shortDescription: The world's largest stock exchange by market capitalization of its listed companies. The building's majestic neoclassical facade, with six Corinthian columns, was designed by George B. Post and opened in 1903.

stops.name: Federal Hall
stops.shortDescription: The site where George Washington took the oath of office as the first President of the United States in 1789. The original building was demolished in 1812, and the current Greek Revival structure was built as a Customs House.

stops.name: Trinity Church
stops.shortDescription: An iconic historic parish church at the intersection of Wall Street and Broadway. Its adjacent graveyard is the final resting place of Alexander Hamilton and other early American prominent figures.

stops.name: 9/11 Memorial
stops.shortDescription: A solemn tribute to the past, featuring two massive reflecting pools set within the footprints of the original Twin Towers. The names of every person who died in the 2001 and 1993 attacks are inscribed into the bronze panels.

stops.name: The Oculus
stops.shortDescription: A striking transportation hub and shopping mall designed by Santiago Calatrava to resemble a dove leaving a child's hands. Its stark white, ribbed structure provides a stark contrast to the surrounding glass skyscrapers.

stops.name: One World Observatory
stops.shortDescription: Located atop One World Trade Center, the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere at a symbolic 1,776 feet. It offers unparalleled views of the New York harbor, the skyline, and beyond.

stops.name: St. Paul's Chapel
stops.shortDescription: The oldest surviving church building in Manhattan, built in 1766. George Washington prayed here on the day of his inauguration, and it miraculously survived the collapse of the nearby Twin Towers.

stops.name: Woolworth Building
stops.shortDescription: Once the tallest building in the world (1913-1930), this stunning neo-Gothic skyscraper is often called the 'Cathedral of Commerce' due to its ornate terra-cotta facade and soaring lobby.

stops.name: City Hall Park
stops.shortDescription: A historic green space surrounding New York City Hall. It has been a site for protests, celebrations, and public gatherings since the American Revolution.

stops.name: Brooklyn Bridge (Manhattan Entrance)
stops.shortDescription: The pedestrian entrance to one of the oldest roadway bridges in the United States. Completed in 1883, its neo-Gothic stone arches and steel suspension cables are an engineering marvel.

stops.name: South Street Seaport
stops.shortDescription: A designated historic district featuring some of the oldest architecture in downtown Manhattan, cobblestone streets, and a collection of historic sailing ships docked along the East River.

stops.name: Pier 17
stops.shortDescription: A modern entertainment and dining destination located right on the water at the Seaport, offering fantastic views of the Brooklyn Bridge and the East River.

stops.name: Stone Street Historic District
stops.shortDescription: One of New York's oldest streets, famously the first to be paved with cobblestones in 1658. Today, it is a lively pedestrian-only alley lined with historic taverns and outdoor dining.

stops.name: Fraunces Tavern
stops.shortDescription: A historic tavern and museum claiming to be the oldest surviving building in Manhattan. It played a prominent role in the Revolution and was where George Washington bid farewell to his officers.
